coredata.dc:title: Electrochemiluminescence sensor for pentoxifylline detection using Au nanoclusters@graphene quantum dots as an amplified electrochemiluminescence luminophore
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xiao-Lei
Li, Xue
Li, Xiao-Tong
Gao, Ye
Feng, Fang
Yang, Gong-Jun
coredata.dc:description: The Au nanoclusters@graphene quantum dots nanocomposite as an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminophore was synthesized through amide coupling reaction between Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The ECL intensity of the as-prepared Au NCs@GQDs was enhanced for 2.6-fold compared with that of Au NCs or 40-fold compared with that of GQDs, which was due to the ECL resonance energy transfer between GQDs as a donor and Au NCs as an acceptor, and excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs. In addition, the synergistic effect likewise improved stability of the ECL signal. A type of “signal-off” solid-state ECL sensor for detection of pentoxifylline was fabricated based on the selective quenching effect of pentoxifylline on the ECL signal of Au NCs@GQDs in alkaline medium. The sensor showed excellent sensitivity and wide linearity for pentoxifylline determination in the range from 7.0 × 10−7 to 1.2 × 10-4 mol/L with the detection limit of 9.0 × 10-8 mol/L (S/N = 3). The proposed method was successfully applied for pentoxifylline detection in pharmaceutical tablet with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Au nanoclusters@graphene quantum dots
Resonance energy transfer
Electrical conductivity
Electrochemiluminescence sensor
Pentoxifylline

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Study of ECL sensor based on Au NCs@GQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Graphical representation of the synthesis of Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite and fabrication of the pentoxifylline ECL sensor.
HRTEM images of Au NCs (A), GQDs (B), Au NCs@GQDs (C), and the EDX elemental mapping images of Au NCs@GQDs (D), (E), (F), (G), (H), (I).
XRD (A) and FT-IR (B) spectra of GQDs (a), Au NCs (b) and Au NCs@GQDs (c).
ECL-time and ECL-potential (inset) curves of GQDs/GCE (a), Au NCs/GCE (b) and Au NCs@GQDs/GCE (c) in 0.1 mol/L NaClO4 containing 70 mmol/L TEA.

(A) Optical absorption spectrum of Au NCs (a) and photoemission spectrum of GQDs (b); (B)Fluorescence decay lines (λex = 365 nm) of GQDs (a) and Au NCs@GQDs (b); (C) EIS curves of bare GCE (a), GQDs/GCE (b), Au NCs@GQDs/GCE (c) and Au NCs/GCE (d).

ECL-time curves of (A) lysozyme-capped Au NCs, (B) HRP-capped Au NCs (a) and HRP-capped Au NCs@GQDs (b), (C) cytochrome c-capped Au NCs (a) and cytochrome c-capped Au NCs@GQDs (b), (D) GSH-capped Au NCs (a) and GSH-capped Au NCs@GQDs (b).

(A) ECL-potential curves of Au NCs@GQDs/GCE for pentoxifylline detection. Pentoxifylline concentration (from curve a to k): 0, 0.70, 3.30, 10.0, 17.0, 23.0, 33.0, 50.0, 66.0, 83.0 and 1.17 × 102 μmol/L in 0.1 mol/L NaClO4 containing 70 mmol/L TEA, respectively, (B) Calibration curve for pentoxifylline detection (n = 4).
Selectivity of the ECL sensor for interferences in electrolyte solution containing 3.3 × 10−6 mol/L of pentoxifylline.
Comparison of analysis methods in pentoxifylline detection.
The recovery of standard addition in pentoxifylline tablet.

head.ce:title: Electrochemiluminescence sensor for pentoxifylline detection using Au nanoclusters@graphene quantum dots as an amplified electrochemiluminescence luminophore

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (China Pharmaceutical University), Ministry of Education, School of Pharmacy, China Pharmaceutical University, Nanjing 210009, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Drug Quality Control and Pharmacovigilance (China Pharmaceutical University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The Au nanoclusters@graphene quantum dots nanocomposite as an electrochemiluminescence (ECL) luminophore was synthesized through amide coupling reaction between Au nanoclusters (Au NCs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The ECL intensity of the as-prepared Au NCs@GQDs was enhanced for 2.6-fold compared with that of Au NCs or 40-fold compared with that of GQDs, which was due to the ECL resonance energy transfer between GQDs as a donor and Au NCs as an acceptor, and excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs. In addition, the synergistic effect likewise improved stability of the ECL signal. A type of “signal-off” solid-state ECL sensor for detection of pentoxifylline was fabricated based on the selective quenching effect of pentoxifylline on the ECL signal of Au NCs@GQDs in alkaline medium. The sensor showed excellent sensitivity and wide linearity for pentoxifylline determination in the range from 7.0 × 10−7 to 1.2 × 10-4 mol/L with the detection limit of 9.0 × 10-8 mol/L (S/N = 3). The proposed method was successfully applied for pentoxifylline detection in pharmaceutical tablet with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Accurate and rapid quantitative determination of drug plays an important role in the drug bioavailability or bioequivalenc, pharmacokinetic study and quality control. Pentoxifylline (PTX) is a hemorheologically active therapeutic agent for the treatment of intermittent claudication [1] and peripheral vascular disease [2]. At present, the common techniques for determination of pentoxifylline mainly included liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) [3], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–7] and gas chromatography (GC) [8]. Although certain chromatographic methods increased the detection sensitivity, these techniques are usually expensive, time consuming and require complicated sample pretreatment procedures. Recently, the electrochemical methods were also used for the detection of pentoxifylline with the advantages of cost-effective, more rapid and convenience based on the electrochemical oxidation of pentoxifylline [9,10].

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL), integrating electrochemistry with chemiluminescence, exhibits many advantages over photoluminescence, such as low background and high sensitivity [11], and it has been extensively applied in many fields, including environment monitoring, medical diagnose and pharmaceutical analysis [12]. Previous common ECL luminophores used to construct sensor mainly include inorganic and organic luminescent reagents, such as ruthenium tris(bipyridine) and luminol, which are not easily immobilized and modified. So these setbacks would influence the stability and sensitivity of sensor. Therefore, it has become a hotspot for taking advantage of various multifunctional nanomaterials to improve the performance of ECL sensors in recent years [13–15]. In ECL sensing, nanomaterials are used not only as ECL emitters but also for signal amplification [16]. To meet the ultrasensitive sensing strategy, the different types of nanomaterials and nanocomposites are designed and synthesized. Besides, nanocomposites reveal more and more properties than single kind of material due to materials synergistic effect mutually. Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer (ECL-RET), as similar with fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), is a non-radiative energy transfer process from an excited state donor to an approximate ground state acceptor [16]. It manifested that the acceptor emission enhanced along with the loss of donor emission [17]. Hence, searching for a pair of applicable donor-acceptor is a promising way to improve the ECL efficiency. For example, Li et al. [18] designed a novel ECL-RET model in which the tris(4,4′-dicarboxylicacid-2,2′-bipyridyl) ruthenium(II) dichloride (Ru(dcbpy)3 2+) (donor) was modified on the surface of CdSe@ZnS quantum dots (QDs) (acceptor). The QDs-Ru(dcbpy)3 2+ nanocomposite used for acting as ECL emitter displayed highly efficient ECL because the short path of energy transmission between donor and acceptor in one nanostructure significantly reduced the energy loss. So it is crucial to choose appropriate donor-acceptor pairs with favorable separation distance, size and shape to improve spectral overlap dimensions and energy transfer for enhancing ECL performance of luminophore [17].

Fluorescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), composed of several to hundreds of Au atoms, were widely applied in biolabeling, biosensing, bioimaging and targeted cancer therapy because of their unusual physicochemical properties [19–21]. It was reported that Au NCs expressed good cathodic [22–25] or anodic [26,27] ECL performance and were used to fabricate ECL sensor. Zhu’s group firstly immobilized Au NCs stabilized by bovine serum albumin (BSA) on the indium tin oxide electrode to construct dopamine ECL sensor using NaS2O8 as a coreagent [22]. Yu et al. [25] employed CoAl layered double hydroxide nanosheets interlaid between blue Au NCs and Ru(bpy)3 2+ to improve the ECL-RET efficiency. Therefore, selecting proper materials to amplify the ECL signal of Au NCs is a kind of particular approach for sensor design. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are small graphene fragments, where electronic transport is confined in all three spatial dimensions [28]. Due to quantum confinement and edge effects, GQDs showed large surface area, high carrier transport mobility, superior mechanical flexibility and excellent thermal/chemical stability. Thus GQDs became the promising material in optoelectronic devices, biosensors and bioimaging applications [29–32]. The margin of GQDs has rich functional carboxyl and hydroxy groups, which can be modified with many kinds of materials. Yu et al. [33] synthesized green-luminescent GQDs functionalized Au@Pt core-shell nanoparticles as ECL nanoprobe to fabricate an enhanced ECL immunosensor. Qiu et al. [34] reported that GQDs were functionalized with peptides through the amide reaction to form peptide/GQDs modified electrode, gaining stable ECL emission signal. Some literatures reported that GQDs were modified with diversified of Au nanomaterials to form new ECL luminophores with the enhancing ECL intensity [35–37]. For example, Yu’s group utilized GQDs loaded surface villous Au nanocages (GQDs@SVAu nanocage) as the ECL signal substance firstly [35]. The specific villous surface structure for Au nanocage showed large specific surface area and excellent electronic transmission efficiency, which permitted more GQDs being carried and electrons transporting. So GQDs@SVAu nanocage displayed higher ECL intensity and lower applied potential and was used to fabricate ECL sensor for CA153 on MCF-7 cell surface. Chi’s group synthesized HM-GQDs-AuNPs nano-hybrids in situ by reducing HAuCl4 with hydrazide-modified graphene quantum dots (HM-GQDs) [36]. The obtained nano-hybrids can be modified easily on the surface of glassy carbon electrodes (GCE), and strong anodic ECL activity was obtained in the presence of H2O2. These papers used Au nanomaterials to strengthen ECL activity of GQDs by taking advantage of excellent electronic transmission efficiency of Au nanomaterials. It showed that the combination of Au nanomaterials and GQDs performed the potential properties.

In this paper, GQDs were used to enhance ECL intensity of BSA-capped Au NCs. As a new type of ECL luminophore, Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite was synthesized through amide coupling reaction between GQDs and BSA-capped Au NCs. The as-prepared nanocomposite showed stronger and more stable anodic ECL emission in comparison to individual Au NCs nanomaterial. It was due to the ECL-RET between GQDs acting as donor and Au NCs acting as acceptor, coupled with high electron conductive properties of GQDs. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), fluorescence spectrometry (FL), ultraviolet and visible (UV–vis) spectrometry and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were employed to characterize the morphology and optical property of Au NCs@GQDs and account for signal enhancement mechanism. Meanwhile, it was found that the luminous intensity of Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite could be quenched by pentoxifylline in basic medium. Accordingly, Au NCs@GQDs were used to act as ECL emission luminophore for constructing a pentoxifylline ECL sensor for the first time. The ECL sensor based on Au NCs@GQDs showed excellent performance, such as wide linear range and low limit of detection, and it could be successfully used for quantitative determination of pentoxifylline sustained-release tablet.

Bovine serum albumin (BSA), N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS), citric acid (CA) and sodium perchlorate (NaClO4) were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation. Nafion (119 solution) was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. l-Glutathione reduced (GSH) was obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. Horseradish peroxidase (HRP), cytochrome c (horse heart) and lysozyme (chinken egg) were obtained from Shanghai Yuan Ye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. 1-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide (EDC) was purchased from Ladder (Shanghai) Chemical Industry Development Co., Ltd. Chloroauric acid (HAuCl4) was obtained from Shanghai Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Triethylamine (TEA) was obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Pentoxifylline was purchased from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Pentoxifylline Sustained-release tablets were purchased from Shijiazhuang Pharmaceutical Group Ouyi Pharmaceutical Co., Ltd. All other chemicals were of analytical-reagent grade. Ultrapure water was used for the experiments.

FT-IR spectrum was obtained on a Nicolet IS-5 spectrophotometer (Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence and UV–vis spectrum were recorded respectively with a Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, Australia) and a Cary 8454 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, USA). Fluorescence decay lines were recorded by a photoluminescence spectromenter 980 (Edinburgh, English). XRD measurement was recorded with a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker-AXS, Germany). HRTEM image was taken using a Tecnai G2 F30 S-Twin TEM (FEI, USA). EIS spectrogram was recorded with a CHI660E workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instruments, China). The ECL signal was detected with a MPI-E Electrochemiluminescence Analyzer (Xi’an Remax Analyse Instrument Co., Ltd.). The conventional three-electrode configuration was used in electrochemical detection, including a modified glassy carbon electrode (GCE, 3 mm in diameter) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode.

The BSA-capped Au NCs were prepared according to the previous work [38]. Briefly, 5 mL of 10 mmol/L HAuCl4 solution was added to 5 mL of 50 mg/mL BSA solution under vigorous stirring at 37℃. After 2 min, 0.5 mL of 1 mol/L NaOH solution was introduced, and the reaction was maintained under vigorous stirring at 37℃ for 12 h. The obtained deep brown Au NCs solution was purified through dialysis with 3500 Da MWCO dialysis bag.

The GQDs were prepared according to the previous method [39]. In brief, 2 g CA was put into a 5 mL breaker and heated at 200℃ for 30 min, then the color of CA liquid was changed to orange, implying the formation of GQDs. The pH of GQDs was neutralized to 7.0 by 100 mL of 10 mg/mL NaOH solution. The obtained GQDs solution was purified via dialysis with 500 Da MWCO dialysis bag.

As shown in Scheme 1 , the Au NCs@GQDs were synthesized through amide coupling reaction between the amino group of Au NCs and the carboxyl group of GQDs. In Au NCs@GQDs synthesis experiment, 50 mg EDC and 50 mg NHS were mixed with 5 mL GQDs solution under stirring for 1 h to activate −COOH group of GQDs. And then 5 mL of Au NCs solution was added to react with GQDs for 12 h under stirring in the dark. The obtained Au NCs@GQDs solution was purified via dialysis with 5000 Da MWCO dialysis bag to clean up free Au NCs and GQDs.

Besides, in accordance with previous reports, other four kinds of organics were used as stabilizer and reductant to synthesize HRP-capped Au NCs [40], GSH-capped Au NCs [41], cytochrome c-capped Au NCs [42] and lysozyme-capped Au NCs [43]. Each organic matter was mixed with HAuCl4 in proportion, NaOH was added to adjust the pH value of the solution, and the reactants were stirred for a while to obtain corresponding organics-capped Au NCs.
The synthesis procedure of HRP-capped Au NCs@GQDs, GSH-capped Au NCs@GQDs and cytochrome c-capped Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite were the same as that of BSA-capped Au NCs@GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of ECL sensor based on Au NCs@GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The GCE was polished with 0.05 μm Al2O3 powder and ultrasonic rinsed with ultrapure water. After polishing, the GCE was rinsed with methanol and dried in nitrogen flow.

The pentoxifylline ECL sensor was fabricated as shown in Scheme 1. Firstly, 40 μL Au NCs@GQDs was mixed uniformly with 10 μL 5% Nafion solution. Then 5 μL of the final solution was dropped onto the clean GCE surface and dried at room temperature.

The performance of ECL sensor based on Au NCs@GQDs modified GCE was investigated at the potential of 0.4–1.1 V (vs SCE) with scan rate of 100 mV/s, and the voltage of the photomultiplier tube was set at 800 V. The 0.1 mol/L NaClO4 containing 70 mmol/L TEA was used as the electrolyte in ECL analysis.

10 tablets, each containing 400 mg/tablet of pentoxifylline, were accurately weighed after the coating being removed and then were grinded finely. To accurately weigh amount of the powder equivalent to approximately 400 mg and place in 200 mL volumetric flask, 100 mL of water was added together. The mixture was heated in a warm water bath and shaken to dissolve. Then the solution was cooled, diluted to volume with water, shaken well and filtered.

The morphology of as-prepared Au NCs, GQDs and Au NCs@GQDs nanomaterials were characterized by HRTEM (Fig. 1 ). The Au NCs were 5 nm approximately (Fig. 1A) and the GQDs nanosheets were less than 5 nm in size (Fig. 1B). Moreover, it can be observed that the lattic spacings were 0.23 nm (Fig. 1A) and 0.28 nm (Fig. 1B) in average, which were consistent with the interplanar spacing of Au NCs [44] and GQDs [45], respectively. The Au NCs and GQDs were chemically cross-linked by covalent reaction between amino of Au NCs and carboxyl of GQDs to synthesize Au NCs@GQDs. Hence, it can be observed that the Au NCs@GQDs (Fig. 1C) became greater in size, which was attributed to that the surface of Au NCs covered by GQDs. The EDX elemental mapping images of the Au NCs@GQDs (Fig. 1D– I) displayed the distribution of Au, S, C, N and O elements contained in the composite materials. These results suggested that the Au NCs@GQDs nanomaterials were successfully synthesized.

Furthermore, the phase structure of Au NCs@GQDs was characterized by XRD and FT-IR. In the XRD patterns of Au NCs@GQDs (curve c of Fig. 2A), a wide (002) peak was observed, which was ascribed to the graphite structure of GQDs [39]. Another two Bragg angles of 31.7° and 45.4° were the characteristic diffraction peaks of Au NCs, which corresponded to the (111) and (200) planes [44]. For FT-IR spectrum in Fig. 2B, the Au NCs@GQDs showed two amide absorption bands at 1653 cm−1 and 1584 cm−1, and the absorption strength and peak position of amideⅡ band changed a bit as compared to that of Au NCs spectrum. Perhaps it is because that the absorption peaks overlapped between CO stretching vibration of GQDs with amideⅡband of Au NCs. Additionally, the absorption of stretching vibration CN at 1240cm−1 and stretching vibration COH at 1090 cm−1 can be observed in the FT-IR spectrum of Au NCs@GQDs. All these results further revealed that the Au NCs@GQDs nanomaterials were successfully synthesized.

The ECL performance was conducted in the range of 0.4 ∼ 1.1 V (vs SCE) as shown in Fig. 3 after the GQDs, Au NCs and Au NCs@GQDs were modified on the surface of GCE, respectively. A strong ECL signal of Au NCs@GQDs modified electrode (inset of Fig. 3c) was observed at ca. 0.97 V, while just a very weak ECL signal was observed on the GQDs modified electrode (inset of Fig. 3a) in 0.1 mol/L NaClO4 with 70 mmol/L TEA. Under the same experimental conditions, although a strong ECL signal of Au NCs/GCE still can be observed (Fig. 3b), the consecutive ECL intensity was not stable. Therefore it implied that Au NCs@GQDs were the luminescent species. In light of the previous reports [26,27], the ECL mechanism of Au NCs@GQDs was through the oxidation and reduction of TEA and Au NCs@GQDs to generate Au NCs@GQDs+ and Au NCs@GQDs−, and then the excited-state Au NCs@GQDs* were generated by electron transfer annihilation. Moreover, the experimental condition of pH value for electrolyte in ECL analysis demonstrated that there were no ECL signals of Au NCs@GQDs in acidic, neutral and weakly alkaline phosphate buffer electrolyte. The results further proved the oxidation of TEA must proceed in a strong alkaline medium. It was also conformed with the ECL mechanism of Au NCs based on the previous literature [27]. The specific process of ECL was possible as follows.
TEA - e → TEA
+
Au NCs@GQDs + TEA
+ → TEA + Au NCs@GQDs+
OH− + TEA
+ → TEA• + H2O
TEA
+ Au NCs@GQDs → TEA+ + Au NCs@GQDs−
Au NCs@GQDs+ + Au NCs@GQDs− → Au NCs@GQDs* + Au NCs@GQDs
Au NCs@GQDs* → Au NCs@GQDs + hv

As shown in Fig. 3b and c, the consecutive ECL intensity of Au NCs@GQDs modified electrode was more stable and stronger than that of Au NCs modified electrode, which was due to the two factors of the ECL-RET between Au NCs and GQDs and the excellent electrical conductivity of GQDs in this work. In Fig. 4 A, the UV absorption spectrum of Au NCs partly-overlapped with the fluorescence spectrum of GQDs (λex = 365 nm). In addition, it was very close distance between Au NCs and GQDs in Au NCs@GQDs because the Au NCs was wrapped with GQDs demonstrated by TEM characterization. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of Au NCs@GQDs decreased compared with that of GQDs as shown in the fluorescence decay lines (Fig. 4B), which manifested the energy transfer between GQDs and Au NCs. Based on the above conditions, the ECL-RET between GQDs as the donor and Au NCs as the acceptor could take place. Besides, the energy loss was reduced in the process of ECL-RET because the Au NCs and GQDs were in one structure. Therefore, the ECL intensity of Au NCs@GQDs was efficiently amplified by means of the ECL energy transfer from GQDs to Au NCs.

To further investigate the effect of GQDs, the electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was used to study three kinds of electrode modified film (Fig. 4C). The semicircle diameter of high frequency region, which was the result of ohmic polarization while the electrons get through the film, represented film impedance in Nyquist plots of EIS. It indicates that the semicircle diameter is smaller while the charge transfer resistance of membrane is smaller. Because of insulation of capped BSA as the stabilizer [38,46], the impedance value of Au NCs modified film was large (shown in Fig. 4C). Owing to the fine electron conduction performance of GQDs, the impedance value of GQDs modified film was much smaller than that of Au NCs. After the hybridization, the impedance value of Au NCs@GQDs modified film decreased markedly. As a result, the fine electron conduction performance of GQDs accelerated the electron delivery in electrochemical luminescence process and enhanced the ECL emission efficiency. And more quantity of Au NCs@GQDs can be absorbed strongly on the surface of GCE due to the high surface area and the graphite structure of GQDs. In conclusion, Au NCs@GQDs composite materials showed more powerful and stable ECL performance in contrast to individual Au NCs nanomaterial.

A variety of organics-capped Au NCs have been reported, so the ECL performance of HRP-capped Au NCs@GQDs, GSH-capped Au NCs@GQDs and cytochrome c-capped Au NCs@GQDs were also studied. Due to the very weak of ECL intensity for lysozyme-capped Au NCs (Fig. 5 A), they were not used to further synthetize lysozyme-capped Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite. It was found that the influence of GQDs on the intensity of other three kinds of Au NCs was unlike with BSA-capped Au NCs. As shown in Fig. 5B and C, the ECL intensity of HRP-capped Au NCs@GQDs and cytochrome c-capped Au NCs@GQDs performed no significant enhancement. The ECL intensity of GSH-capped Au NCs@GQDs decreased unexpectedly in comparison to that of GSH-capped Au NCs as shown in Fig. 5D. Several studies have reported that the properties of stabilizer protecting Au atoms would also affect some of the physicochemical properties of Au NCs [47]. We speculated that the structure difference of stabilizer capped on Au atoms influenced the ECL properties of AuNCs and corresponding nanocomposite. The tripeptide GSH is a small molecule structure, but the BSA, HRP and cytochrome c are macromolecule with different structure. When they reacted with GQDs to form nanocomposites, the surface states of Au NCs@GQDs was diverse, such as geometrical structure and spatial distance between Au NCs and GQDs, and all these would make nanomaterials to display different ECL performance. However, the ECL stability of three kinds of nanocomposites was all improved. In consequence, the BSA-capped Au NCs@GQDs composite materials were chosen as the amplified ECL luminophore to fabricate ECL sensor for the detection of pentoxifylline.

The reproducibility of sensor was evaluating by using five Au NCs@GQDs modified electrodes to test pentoxifylline at equal concentrations. The RSD (n = 5) for measurement of pentoxifylline was 1.3%, which suggested that the propose ECL sensor for pentoxifylline performed good reproducibility.

An interference investigation was performed by using the solution containing 3.3 × 10−6 mol/L of pentoxifylline and other common components in pharmaceutical formulation and body fluid respectively (Fig. 7 ). It showed that more than 1000-fold of K+, Na+, Cl-, 100-fold of Mg2+, ascorbic acid (AA), citric acid (CA), alanine (Ala), glycine (Gly), urea, 10-fold of uric acid (UA), lysine (Lys), phenylalanine (Phe), and equal concentration of dopamine (DA), cysteine (Cys), glutathine (GSH), glucose, Hg2+, Cu2+ had almost no influence on the ECL response. Those amino acid, AA, CA, etc. carry negative charge under pH 11.5 electrolyte solution, so the Nafion film on the surface of electrode can reject them.

In order to evaluate the feasibility in analysis of real samples, the proposed ECL sensor was applied to determine pentoxifylline content in tablet. The content of pentoxifylline was 382.95 mg/tablet with a relative standard deviation (RSD) 3.2%, (n = 5). This value was closed to 382.51 mg/tablet with RSD of 2.0% (n = 5) and 381.96 mg/tablet with RSD of 0.2% (n = 5) obtained using the method in China Pharmacopoeia [52] and HPLC method [7], respectively. To further evaluate the accuracy of the ECL sensor, the recovery of pentoxifylline for the tablet sample was determined by the standard addition method. The recovery was listed in Table 2 . The recovery of pentoxifylline samples ranged from 99.70% to 106.61% with RSD at less than 4.8% using ECL method, while the recovery of 97.60% to 102.70% with RSD at less than 0.75% was obtained using HPLC method. These results demonstrated that the ECL sensor was suitable for determining the pentoxifylline content in tablet.

In conclusion, the BSA-capped Au NCs were covalent linkage with GQDs via amide bond to form Au NCs@GQDs nanocomposite. In virtue of synergistic effect, the as-prepared Au NCs@GQDs displayed more stable and stronger ECL performance due to ECL-RET and unique physical chemistry and structure properties of doped GQDs. Therefore, the fabricated ECL sensor based on the quenching effect of pentoxifylline on signal of Au NCs@GQDs presented wide linearity range, low detection limit and preferable selectivity in pentoxifylline determination. In addition, the ECL sensor has also been successfully applied to determine pentoxifylline in tablet sample, which has a promising potential for applications in accurate and rapid quantitative drug determination.
There is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Clinical pharmacology of pentoxifylline with special reference to its hemorheologic effect for the treatment of intermittent claudication
Use of pentoxifylline as an inhibitor of free radical generation in peripheral vascular disease
Bioanalysis of pentoxifylline and related metabolites in plasma samples through LC-MS/MS
Simple high-performance liquid chromatographic method for determination of pentoxifylline in human plasma

Rapid high-performance liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry method for determination of pentoxifylline and its active metabolites M1 and M5 in human plasma and its application in bioavailability study
Simultaneous estimation of lisofylline and pentoxifylline in rat plasma by high performance liquid chromatography-photodiode array detector and its application to pharmacokinetics in rat
Optimization and validation of a new HPLC method using monolithic column for simultaneous determination of pentoxifylline and related compounds
Study of the solid phase extraction of pentoxifylline and its major metabolite as a basis of their rapid low concentration gas chromatographic determination in serum
Electrochemical oxidation of pentoxifylline and its analysis in pure and pharmaceutical formulations at a glassy carbon electrode
Electrochemical determination of a hemorheologic drug, pentoxifylline at a multi-walled carbon nanotube paste electrode
Current trends in the development of the electrochemiluminescent immunosensors
Recent advances based on nanomaterials as electrochemiluminescence probes for the fabrication of sensors
Recent advances in electrochemiluminescence with quantum dots and arrays of nanoelectrodes
Controlling the spectroscopic properties of quantum dots via energy transfer and charge transfer interactions: concepts and applications
Highly efficient electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer system in one nanostructure: its application for ultrasensitive detection of microRNA in cancer cells
Recent advances in biomedical applications of fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Fluorescent gold nanoclusters: synthesis and recent biological application
Electrogenerated chemiluminescence of Au nanoclusters for the detection of dopamine
Fabrication of a dispersible graphene/gold nanoclusters hybrid and its potential application in electrogenerated chemiluminescence
An electrogenerated chemiluminescence sensor prepared with a graphene/multiwall carbon nanotube/gold nanocluster hybrid for the determination of phenolic compounds
Gold nanoclusters@Ru(bpy)3 2+-layered double hydroxide ultrathin film as a cathodic electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer probe
A novel solid-state electrochemiluminescence sensor for the determination of hydrogen peroxide based on an Au nanocluster–silica nanoparticle nanocomposite
Graphene quantum dots: an emerging material for energy-related applications and beyond
Graphene quantum dots: emergent nanolights for bioimaging, sensors, catalysis and photovoltaic devices
Focusing on luminescent graphene quantum dots: current status and future perspectives
Cyclam-functionalized carbon dots sensor for sensitive and selective detection of copper(II) ion and sulfide anion in aqueous media and its imaging in live cells
Real-time monitoring of endogenous cysteine levels in living cells using a CD-based ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe
Paper-based electrochemiluminescence immunodevice for carcinoembryonic antigen using nanoporous gold-chitosan hybrids and graphene quantum dots functionalized Au@Pt
Electrochemiluminescence resonance energy transfer between graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide for sensitive protein kinase activity and inhibitor sensing
Electrochemiluminescence device for in-situ and accurate determination of CA153 at the MCF-7 cell surface based on graphene quantum dots loaded surface villous Au nanocage
Immobilizing water-soluble graphene quantum dots with gold nanoparticles for low potential electrochemiluminescence immunosensor
A graphene quantum dots based electrochemiluminescence immunosensor for carcinoembryonic antigen detection using poly(5-formylindole)/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposite
Protein-directed synthesis of highly fluorescent gold nanoclusters
Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots and graphene oxide prepared by tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid
Horseradish peroxidase functionalized fluorescent gold nanoclusters for hydrogen peroxide sensing
Near-Infrared electrochemiluminescence from orange fluorescent Au nanoclusters in water
Cytochrome c-capped fluorescent gold nanoclusters: imaging of live cells and delivery of cytochrome c
Lysozyme-stabilized gold nanoclusters as a novel fluorescence probe for cyanide recognition
Synthesizing a nano-composite of BSA-capped Au nanoclusters/graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets as a new florescent probe for dopamine detection
In-situ synthesis and ultrasound enhanced adsorption properties of MoS2/graphene quantum dot nanocomposite
Highly selective and ultrasensitive detection of Hg2+ based on florescence quenching of Au nanoclusters by Hg2+–Au+ interactions
Roles of thiolate ligands in the synthesis, properties and catalytic application of gold nanoclusters
Determination of pentoxifylline in serum by highperformance thin-layer chromatography
Determination of pentoxifylline in pharmaceutical formulations using iodine as oxidizing agent
Spectrofluorimetric determination of drugs containing active methylene group using N1-methyl nicotinamide chloride as a fluorigenic agent
New validated potentiometric determination of vasodilator pentoxifylline in its pharmaceutical formulations and biological fluids
Pharmacopoeia of PR China Part Ⅱ

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiao-Lei Zhang is now pursuing her PhD degree at China Pharmaceutical University. Her research interest is electrochemiluminescene sensor for pharmaceutical analysis.
Xue Li is a MS student at China Pharmaceutical University.
Xiao-Tong Li is a MS student at China Pharmaceutical University.
Ye Gao is a MS student at China Pharmaceutical University.

Fang Feng has a PhD in Pharmaceutical analysis obtained at China Pharmaceutial University in 2003. She is a professor of Pharmaceutiacl Analysis. Her research work has been mainly focused on the development of new analytical methods for complex systems.

Gong-Jun Yang has a PhD in analytical chemistry obtained at Nanjing University in 2004. He is a professor of Analytical Chemistry. His research work has been mainly focused on the design of new electrochemical sensors.
